summary: DHH reflects on how the HEY World team spent excessive time worrying about potential abuse that never materialized. Despite building elaborate defenses and debating complex policies, the abuse reporting link was never used for a legitimate violation. He argues this disproportionate worry about phantom threats is a common trap for businesses, advocating instead for a 'it's a problem when it's a problem' approach that addresses issues only when they actually arise.
key_points: HEY World's abuse reporting link was never used for an actual violation despite thousands of posts, proving the team's fears were unfounded
The team wasted energy designing delayed-access regimes, support trauma protocols, and policy debates for threats that never materialized
Two of the five links on each minimal HEY World page were dedicated to abuse prevention — a disproportionate allocation driven by disproportionate worry
Context matters for risk assessment: paying customers, no amplification, and abundant alternatives for bad actors made mass abuse unlikely
For most businesses, the real threat isn't the bad things that happen — it's the energy spent on the ones that never do
money_quotes: All that worry, all that consternation, all those arguments back and forth about policy and protocols, all for nothing.
So 2/5 of all the links on the page were reserved to deal with the phantom menace of abuse we worried ourselves into believing would arrive.
That's not proportionate outcome because it was not a proportionate process.

We also discussed a convoluted regime by which even paying customers would have delayed access to HEY World, because what if a bunch of people would pay us, just to spout hate from these pages in massive numbers?
For most small- and even medium-sized businesses, the main threat is not the few bad things that eventually happen. It's spending all your attention and energy on the ones that never do.
key_insight: Disproportionate worry about unlikely threats wastes more resources than the threats themselves ever would, and businesses should default to solving problems only when they actually materialize.
